title: New York Fed Report Shows Household Debt Rises in Third Quarter

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's Household Debt and Credit Report for Q3 2013 showed total household debt rose $127 billion to $11.28 trillion, the largest quarterly increase since Q1 2008. The increase was driven by rises in mortgage balances ($56B), student loans ($33B), auto loans ($31B), and credit card debt ($4B). Senior economist Donghoon Lee noted households appear to have crossed a turning point in the deleveraging cycle. The 90+ day delinquency rate fell to 5.3%, the lowest since Q3 2008.
key_points: Total household indebtedness reached $11.28 trillion in Q3 2013, up $127 billion from Q2 2013
This was the largest quarterly increase since Q1 2008, suggesting end of deleveraging cycle
90+ day delinquency rate declined to 5.3%, lowest since Q3 2008
Student loan delinquency rate increased to 11.8% from 10.9% quarter-over-quarter
Auto loan balances increased for 10 consecutive quarters, reaching $845 billion
New foreclosure notations dropped to 168,000, down 70% from Q2 2009 peak of 566,000
why_it_matters: The report signals a potential turning point in U.S. household deleveraging, with broad-based increases in borrowing across all debt categories. This has implications for consumer spending capacity, financial sector asset quality, and monetary policy normalization timing. Rising student loan delinquency rates indicate structural stress in that sector despite overall improving credit conditions.
macro_context: Household debt recovery suggests improving consumer sentiment and spending potential. Total debt remains 11% below the Q3 2008 peak of $12.68 trillion. Rising student loan delinquencies (11.8%) indicate persistent education debt stress. Improving credit quality broadly suggests strengthening household balance sheets.
market_structure_context: This source does not address market structure (trading mechanisms, market plumbing, or operational microstructure). It is a household debt and credit trends report from the New York Fed, more appropriately classified under macro catalysts.
important_numbers: $11.28 trillion - total household debt in Q3 2013
